name: Outer Wilds
Description:
Outer Wilds has won another major award, being named "Game of the Year 2019" by Giant Bomb, Polygon, Eurogamer, and The Guardian! The acclaimed and award-winning Outer Wilds is an open-world adventure game set in a solar system trapped in an endless time loop.
Welcome to the space program!
You are the newest member of the Outer Wilds adventure, a rookie space program set out to find answers in a strange, ever-evolving solar system.
The mysteries of the solar system...
What lurks at the heart of the ominous planet Dark Bramble? Who built the alien ruins on the moon? Can the endless time loop be stopped? The answers await you in the most dangerous regions of space.
A world that changes over time
The planets of Outer Wilds are filled with hidden locations that change over time. Visit an underground city before it's swallowed by sand, or explore the surface of a planet as it crumbles beneath your feet. Each secret is protected by dangerous environments and natural disasters.
Bring your interstellar hiking gear!
Lace up your hiking boots, check your oxygen levels, and get ready for a space adventure. Use a variety of unique gadgets to explore your surroundings, track mysterious signals, decipher ancient alien script, and bake the perfect marshmallow.
requirements:
Minimum requirements:
Requires a 64-bit processor and operating system.
Operating System: Windows 10
Processor: Intel Core i5-2300 | AMD FX-4350
Memory: 6 GB RAM
Graphics cards: Nvidia GeForce GTX 660, 2 GB | AMD Radeon HD 7870, 2 GB
Storage space: Requires 8 GB of available space
Additional notes: Gamepad or Controller recommended (Xbox and PS4 natively supported)
Recommended configuration:
Requires a 64-bit processor and operating system.
Operating System: Windows 10
Processor: Intel Core i5-8400 | AMD Ryzen 5 2600X
Memory: 8 GB RAM
Graphics cards: Nvidia GeForce GTX 1060 | AMD Radeon RX 580
Storage space: Requires 8 GB of available space
Additional notes: Gamepad or Controller recommended (Xbox and PS4 natively supported)
Comment1:
May the stars inspire your imagination; with stars above, may their light accompany you on your glorious journey. Even those stars you cannot see will forever illuminate your path.
A first-person, non-linear, open-world exploration game. It's a very impressive game, but I was just too clumsy and kept getting stuck because I couldn't figure things out. Later, I looked at a full walkthrough, which ruined the experience. Even so, Outer Wilds is still a great game. Even with a full walkthrough, it's definitely worth playing through again. The music is also great; you'd think you were in Stardew Valley. The story is driven by exploration, creating a strong sense of immersion. The player experiences being a protagonist in a sci-fi universe, driven by a thirst for adventure, exploring the deep, vast, and perilous cosmos. By exploring each planet, you uncover the truth of the universe, perfectly fulfilling most players' fantasies about the cosmos. It's the pinnacle of sci-fi universe games, with no power-ups or tedious tasks, just pure exploration. The moment you piece together the truth is incredibly satisfying. But what makes the universe so alluring isn't the answers, but the realization, even as you painstakingly arrive at them, that it's just the tip of the iceberg. Even so, humanity has never stopped exploring the universe since ancient times; perhaps it's human nature.
This game doesn't have much quest guidance; players mainly rely on their own exploration to find the truth. There's no fixed process, so it's strongly recommended not to rely too much on video guides. If you're really confused or stuck and need to check a guide, just take a quick look at the path and explore the rest of the way yourself. Otherwise, you'll regret watching so many guides in the first place.
In this near-infinite cycle, you will reach the end of the truth. Pay attention, this is how death return is used! This game has a mechanism that forces a reset of exploration. Even if the player does nothing, after a period of time, they will be reset to the starting point due to some events. The clues that have been explored will be retained in the next cycle, and the true ending will be reached in the continuous cycle.
The interactive elements that align with the player's intuition, rather than just dry modeling as a background, are a pleasant surprise. The guidance is weak, but it greatly stimulates the player's desire to explore. The exploration process is also a learning process for the player. It is not the character that becomes stronger, but the player themselves.
The only thing I'm not satisfied with is the motion sickness issue. After constantly modifying the graphics settings, I reduced the blur to the minimum, lowered all the lighting and shadow effects to the lowest level, set the field of view to around 60 degrees, enabled V-Sync, and kept the resolution at 1080p, which effectively alleviated the motion sickness problem. However, I still felt dizzy. It's a bit of a pity that players who are prone to motion sickness can't fully enjoy the game in one go.
Comment2:
To be honest, I first played Outer Wilds last year. Back then, I was prone to motion sickness from 3D games, and after wandering around the village, I felt like I was about to die, so I immediately shut down the game. I have to say, I was seriously discouraged at the time: the mysterious jumping controls, the model spaceships that would crash as soon as I started playing, even playing hide-and-seek with kids made me feel frustrated... I couldn't help but wonder, what kind of design made this game a masterpiece in the eyes of so many people?
Until this year, I opened the game again. This time, I no longer get motion sickness from 3D, and I quickly obtained the launch code by following the path I remembered, and flew into space. Coincidentally, as soon as I entered space, I saw trees on the moon and a blurry figure of a Hassian. So I felt what is called curiosity—I wanted to meet my companions in the solar system.
Outer Wilds offers virtually no guidance. The entire game is driven by the player's curiosity. There are no restrictions; go wherever you want and explore the entire solar system at will. I scanned planet after planet with my signal scope, meeting friend after friend. This should just be a simple exploration game, I thought.
Until one day, I heard the background music that made all Outer Endurance players incredibly nervous, and saw a scene that I will probably never forget for the rest of my life (may contain very minor spoilers), and I realized that the game had truly begun.
If my initial exploratory mindset was merely that of a tourist, it transformed into an overwhelming thirst for knowledge. I wanted to know why; this solar system truly held so many unanswered questions. I began to seize every moment to delve deeper into each planet, studying the changes they underwent over time, racking my brains to decipher the spaceship's logbook, and, of course, enduring the torment of certain jumping puzzles. Gradually, connections formed between the logbooks, my purpose for exploration became clearer, and the story of the entire solar system, and even the entire universe, slowly began to surface.
Simply stating this truth wouldn't be particularly impactful. But in Outer Wilds, it's something players piece together, following their curiosity and piecing together fragments of information from dilapidated buildings. Only through firsthand experience can one truly understand how captivating, precious, and romantic this feeling is. This story is so romantic that even just opening the game, sitting by a campfire baking a marshmallow, fills me with a complex mix of emotions, deeply moved by everything that unfolds.
Being able to play this game is truly one of the greatest joys of my life. If I could, I would like to erase my memories and relive this incredibly romantic story.
After finishing Outer Wilds, whenever I'm daydreaming or browsing my game library, I often think of:
"I probably won't be able to play games like this again."
Comment3:
5/10
The music is great, the planetary scenery is nice and interesting, but there's not much else to say. As for the plot, let's make the game playable before talking about this quantum state stuff.

As for what Brother Bi said, you'll see the comments to understand why I rate this "game" this way.

It's boring. Don't care how much others praise it or how good the reviews are; this game is just boring. If you don't believe me, look at the community reviews; without exception, they're all just vague phrases like "curiosity." Why? It's because these niche fans can only find solace in this kind of self-deprecating humor, like art maggots. The things they define are just a way to express their superiority.

As a game, playability is of course the most important thing. Similarly, Zelda, a game driven by curiosity, adds other points of interest to your intermittent curiosity. This game is like doing a math problem. At most, you can see how the problem is at the beginning, but after a while, you will find that you have no clue and you will basically lose all motivation to continue.
No matter how artistically brilliant a game is, if it lacks basic gameplay, it's not a game. You can adapt it into other media—novels, films, comics—but it can never be a game. Not to mention the guidance is provided through the garbage medium of "text," forcing me to do a short reading every time I want to explore a planet. The character and spaceship controls are comparable to Death Stranding—uniquely terrible. The puzzles are basically just guesswork and sheer luck; the design is utterly awful.

As for those who rave about this game, they're really amazing. They've even come up with Chinese phrases that I've never heard before. But when I ask them what they think of the game, all they say is, "You're just curious, you don't understand." This game is full of niche, indie "Brahmins." Honestly, if the game's plot were truly amazing, they wouldn't care about spoilers. The game is just boring, and they can only compensate by focusing on other aspects that are hard to judge at first glance.

The game's popularity in China (though not exceptionally high) is largely due to bandwagon effects and the influence of Brother B.
